conclusions.description: The basalt shaft in the blast chamber is a seal predating recorded history, stoppered by ice since before the last Ice Age.
conclusions.fallback_policy: If all clue routes missed, the shelter survivor's folklore (Keeper may prompt an INT x5) supplies the 'sealed since ancient times' detail.

clues.player_safe_summary: The shaft's inner walls are lined with black basalt, not native to these mountains; anything dropped into it produces no echo at all.
Drag marks and a blood trail lead from the carnage straight to the shaft's mouth — something pulled bodies down into it.
The Austrians were widening the chamber when they blew away a thick layer of ice capping a hole in the rock floor — the shaft wasn't dug, it was unsealed.
A local folk tradition holds that something ancient sleeps beneath the highest glaciers, sealed since before the long winter that covered the world.

conclusions.description: The Austrian demolition work blew open the ice seal and unwittingly released the entity from the shaft.
conclusions.fallback_policy: If routes missed, the timing (explosions nights ago, then silence) is stated plainly by the briefing officer on recall.

clues.player_safe_summary: The company commander lets slip that the Austrians reported strange sounds and muffled explosions in the days before contact was lost — before everything on that ridge went quiet.
The killing ground that should be alive with rifle fire is dead calm — no shots, no shouts, no birds, the sort of hush that follows something having cleared a place out.
The machine-gun posts were abandoned in a hurry — loaded guns, drifted-over muzzles, wandering tracks leading back into the tunnels, none of it an orderly withdrawal.
One or two Schwarzlose guns were wrenched around to fire back into the Austrian positions — they weren't fighting the Italians.
Deeper in the glacier honeycomb the drag marks resume — the same wide grooves as the blood trail, heading steadily down toward the blast chamber.
Dropped packs and scattered ammunition stud the tunnel floor — the Austrians threw off their own kit as they fled, unbothered by any Italian pursuit.
The blast chamber is plastered with the torn bodies of Austrians, weapons still clutched, spent casings underfoot — they fought back against whatever came.
The wounds were inflicted by superhuman strength and huge serrated edges — no blast or bullet did this.

conclusions.description: The entity is a Polyp Horror, one of the subterranean adversaries of the prehistoric Great Race, lingering underground for geological ages.
conclusions.fallback_policy: If missed, this is purely flavor; no mechanic depends on the investigators naming it.

clues.player_safe_summary: The thing probes and discards victims rather than simply killing — it is examining humanity, not hunting for food.
The gale that erupts on the saddle rises and falls with the pursuer's advance — the wind isn't driving the thing, the thing is the wind, or close enough to make no difference.
The whistling moves. It approaches from the direction of the shaft and follows the patrol — it isn't wind.

conclusions.description: The entity avoids direct daylight, retreating to shadow at dawn — the key to any counterstroke.
conclusions.fallback_policy: If missed, dawn breaking in scene shelter-night-siege makes the entity's withdrawal behavior obvious regardless.

clues.player_safe_summary: At first light the entity withdraws to the shadows at the mountain's base, holding its whirl-of-snow form and avoiding direct sun.
In daylight the entity's reactions are sluggish — it suffers a clear penalty and needs a moment's preparation to summon its wind again.

conclusions.description: Brought to 20 HP or below, the entity abandons its curiosity and retreats down the shaft to heal.
conclusions.fallback_policy: Keeper may state this plainly if the patrol has damaged it significantly.

clues.delivery: observing the entity's behavior under fire
clues.player_safe_summary: Once badly hurt, the entity decides further study isn't worth the risk and flees for the shaft, killing only those in its way.

conclusions.description: A triggered avalanche's mass impact can wound the entity where weapons cannot — it is semi-material, not immune to physics.
conclusions.fallback_policy: If missed, the heavily-loaded snow overhang above the shelter is an obvious environmental feature the Keeper can draw attention to.

clues.delivery: noticing the snow overhang above the shelter
clues.player_safe_summary: A heavy overhang of snow has built up on the summit ridge above the shelter — plainly visible and plainly unstable, a grave avalanche risk.

conclusions.description: Whatever the patrol does, the next day's catastrophic White Friday avalanches will come — but who is blamed depends on their choices.
conclusions.fallback_policy: The disaster is historical and inevitable; the conclusion is resolved in the aftermath scene regardless of clue discovery.

clues.player_safe_summary: On 13 December 1916 the White Friday disaster kills some 10,000 on both sides. If the patrol left the entity alive, the glacier is buried regardless; if they triggered an avalanche themselves, they shoulder the blame; only killing the entity frees them of it.
